\begin{questions}
  \question[4] 
  Fill in the blanks below using the correct big-O notation for each case:\vspace{1.5ex}
  \begin{parts}
    \part If $f = O(h^3)$ and $g = O(h^3)$, then $f + g = O(h^3)$.\vspace{1.5ex}
    \part If $f = O(h^3)$ and $g = O(h^3)$, then $f - g = O(h^3)$.\vspace{1.5ex}
    \part If $f = O(h^3)$ and $g = O(h^2)$, then $f + g = O(h^2)$.\vspace{1.5ex}
    \part If $f = O(h^3)$ and $g = O(h^2)$, then $f - g = O(h^2)$.
  \end{parts}
  where we are interested in the behavior as $h \to 0$.
  \question[8]
  Construct a finite difference approximation for the first derivative $f'(x)$ at a point $x$ using the points $x + h$, and $x - 2h$. What is the order of accuracy of your approximation?

  \begin{solution}
    We seek a finite difference for $f'(x)$ using $f(x-h)$ and $f(x+2h)$. Assume
    \[
      f'(x) \approx a\, f(x-h) + b\, f(x+2h)
    \]
    Expand via Taylor series:
    \begin{align*}
      f(x+h) &= f(x) + h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2} f''(x) + O(h^3) \\
      f(x-2h) &= f(x) - 2h f'(x) + 2h^2 f''(x) + O(h^3)
    \end{align*}
    Combine and match coefficients to isolate $f'(x)$:
    \[
      a + b = 0, \quad a - 2b = \frac{1}{h}
    \]
    Solving gives $a = \frac{1}{3h}$, $b = -\frac{1}{3h}$, so
    \[
      f'(x) \approx \frac{-f(x-2h) + f(x+h)}{3h}
    \]
    Substituting the expansions, the leading error is $O(h)$.

    \textbf{Final answer:}
    \[
      \boxed{
        f'(x) \approx \frac{-f(x-2h) + f(x+h)}{3h}
      }
      \qquad \text{with error } O(h)
    \]
  \end{solution}

  \newpage

  \question[8]
  Improve the accuracy of the following finite difference approximation you derived in the previous question by using Richardson extrapolation. Simplify completely and provide a new finite difference approximation for $f'(x)$ and state the order of accuracy of your new approximation.

  \begin{solution}
    Let $D(h) = \frac{-f(x-2h) + f(x+h)}{3h}$ be the original approximation. Compute $D(2h)$:
    \[
      D(2h) = \frac{-f(x-4h) + f(x+2h)}{6h}
    \]
    The error expansions are:
    \begin{align*}
      D(h) &= f'(x) + C h + O(h^2) \\
      D(2h) &= f'(x) + C (2h) + O(h^2)
    \end{align*}
    Using Richardson extrapolation:
    \[
      R = 2 D(h) - D(2h) = 2\left(f'(x) + C h\right) - \left(f'(x) + 2C h\right) + O(h^2)
    \]
    Simplifying gives:
    \[
      R = f'(x) + O(h^2)
    \]
    Thus, the new approximation is:
    \[
      \boxed{
        f'(x) \approx 2\frac{-f(x-2h) + f(x+h)}{3h} - \frac{-f(x-4h) + f(x+2h)}{6h}
      }
      \qquad \text{with error } O(h^2)
    \]

      Note: You can also directly state that the Richardson extrapolation for an estimate of order $O(h)$ is given by $R = 2 D(h) - D(2h)$, which improves the order from $O(h)$ to $O(h^2)$.  
  \end{solution}


  \newpage


\end{questions}
